Title: Taste of home in Makkah as ex-footballer serves Malaysian dishes to pilgrims
Article:
MAKKAH: Who would have thought that in the heart of the holy city of Makkah, Malaysian pilgrims can still savour nasi lemak, nasi kerabu, nasi dagang and a variety of local dishes that taste just like home throughout their haj journey.
Behind these appetising dishes is former national footballer Hairuddin Omar, who has ventured into the food business in Makkah to bring Malaysian flavours closer to pilgrims.
He said the idea of opening a Malaysian food café in Rehab Janadriyah, about 1.7km from Masjidil Haram, came from observing how pilgrims often long for Malaysian cuisine while performing haj.
"When they are here for a long time, they will certainly miss Malaysian food. So I want to offer something that makes them feel closer to home," he said when met here.
He said the café caters entirely to Malaysian tastes, ensuring every dish is prepared using authentic recipes and quality ingredients.
Among the popular menu items are nasi lemak, nasi kerabu, nasi dagang, asam pedas, lontong and roti canai, dishes that are hard to find in the surrounding area, making them a preferred choice for pilgrims to start their day.
To preserve authenticity, Hairuddin brings in three chefs from Malaysia and prepares the food at a central kitchen in Kudai before delivering it to the café.
During the haj season, in addition to operating the café, he also supplies food to several hotels housing thousands of Malaysian pilgrims, allowing more of them to enjoy home-style meals.
He said the main focus is breakfast, as most haj packages only provide lunch and dinner.
"Once food is taken care of, pilgrims are less burdened and can fully focus on their worship," he said.
At the same time, he acknowledged facing various challenges, including compliance with Saudi Arabia's food safety regulations, logistical issues and sourcing raw ingredients, all of which require careful planning.
"We bring some of the spices from Malaysia to ensure the taste remains truly authentic and helps ease the pilgrims' homesickness," he said.
The café operates from as early as 4am until 10pm, with its schedule tailored to the pilgrims' daily routine, especially after Subuh prayers when breakfast demand surges.
For Hairuddin, the effort is not merely a business, but a form of service to pilgrims who come to fulfil the fifth pillar of Islam, while bringing a taste of home to the holy land.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Health Ministry orders comprehensive structural, digital audit of Ampang Hospital
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Health Ministry (MOH) has directed a comprehensive structural and digital audit of Ampang Hospital to address underlying issues stemming from its ageing infrastructure.
In a post on his X account yesterday, Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said the ministry is moving with urgency to restore normalcy, stressing that patient safety remains a non-negotiable priority.
On the disruption of the hospital's electronic Hospital Information System (eHIS), Dzulkefly said it was caused by a technical maintenance error that affected system modules.
"Technical teams from the ministry and principals are currently performing system synchronisation and data integrity validation. Business Continuity Plans (BCP) are active to ensure clinical care continues manually without interruption," he said after visiting the hospital today to oversee remedial actions following recent digital and infrastructure disruptions.
Regarding the facility's diagnostic capacity, he said the CT scan replacement project is nearing completion. Following a transition to a new leasing model, the unit is on track to be operational by May 1.
He added that MOH is currently reviewing procurement protocols to ensure such transition gaps are eliminated moving forward.
Commenting on the hospital's infrastructure integrity, the minister said a recent leakage in the operating theatre (OT) complex involved a chilled water pipe within the ventilation system.
"While repairs were completed swiftly, we are now conducting mandatory re-sterilisation, terminal cleaning and air-particle audits. There will be no compromise; the OT will only resume once 100 per cent infection control compliance is verified," he said.
Dzulkefly also expressed his gratitude to Hospital Ampang frontliners for their dedication and resilience in managing the recent challenges, reaffirming the government's commitment to building a more robust and fail-safe healthcare infrastructure.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Woman's resilience shines as she cares for autistic son, paralysed spouse
Article:
DENGKIL: Thirteen years ago, Aishah Mohamed Amin and her husband, Mohd Shafiq Mohd Som, began a journey of patient endurance when their eldest son, Juhd, was born with autism.
However, life dealt the family another devastating blow in August 2023 when Shafiq suffered a massive stroke.
The brain haemorrhage, caused by high blood pressure and genetic factors, left the former freelance illustrator paralysed on the left side of his body.
Today, the man who was once the family's provider and a devoted father remains unable to care for himself.
"He was in a coma for four weeks in the ICU. He survived, but everything changed after that," Aishah said when met at her home in Kampung Sungai Buah here.
The transition from wife to full-time caregiver for both a disabled child and a bedridden husband took a severe toll on Aishah's mental health.
She admitted to going through a prolonged period of denial and emotional strain, which eventually led to a clinical diagnosis of Major Depressive Disorder and anxiety distress.
"I had to learn to accept God's will, even though I felt a profound sense of loss since he fell ill," she said.
Aishah's daily routine involves balancing her husband's intensive medical needs, including hygiene and physiotherapy, with the demands of raising four school-going children.
Her afternoons are spent juggling household chores and hospital appointments at Hospital Serdang or Hospital Kuala Lumpur for both her husband and autistic son.
Despite her exhausting schedule, Aishah works from home as a content creator, producing promotional videos for children's books published by her family's business.
The medical outlook, however, remains bleak, with doctors recently classifying Shafiq's condition as palliative.
"He is immobile now, and I care for him more like a mother than a wife. It's lonely, but support from family and friends keeps me going," she said.
Aishah now documents her journey on Instagram, using the platform to preserve memories for her husband and offer support to fellow caregivers.
"I'm not actually that strong, but I am grateful for the support. I want other caregivers to know they are not alone," she said.
The family currently receives a RM500 monthly caregiver allowance from the Social Welfare Department, as well as basic necessities from the Selangor Zakat Board.
Her eldest son also receives a RM1,800 annual allowance for students with special needs from the Education Ministry.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: CCTV footage shows man attempting break-in at Sungai Petani home
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A closed-circuit television (CCTV) recording of an unknown man attempting to enter a house in Taman Cendana, Sungai Petani, Kedah, has gone viral.
The footage, shared on a community
Threads account (@penduduksgpetani),
shows the man standing at the gate before entering the house compound. The incident is believed to have occurred about 10.30am.
According to the homeowner, the incident took place shortly after his wife returned home.
"After dropping off our child, my wife stopped briefly to run an errand before returning home to collect some work items. She was only working a half-day and it was around 10.30am.
"As she intended to be home only briefly, she did not lock the gate or the grill, which she normally does.
"About five minutes later, an unknown man came from the field in front of our house. He opened the gate and entered the compound.
"He then tried to open the grill and even banged on the main door. Fortunately, the door was locked, so he could not gain entry. Otherwise, we do not know what might have happened."
The homeowner said he was at work when his wife called, prompting him to rush home.
He added that the suspect fled after failing to gain entry.
"I later checked the car dashcam and it clearly showed that he had already been at the field in front of the house when my wife returned earlier," he said.
A police report has since been lodged.
"According to the police, he was likely attempting to commit theft or another offence. Lesson learnt – we will ensure all doors are locked at all times," he added.
Several social media users also shared their concerns, with some suggesting the suspect may have been observing the house beforehand, while others recounted similar incidents involving daylight break-ins.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ex-FBI director Comey appears in court over Trump threat charge
Article:
WASHINGTON, (United States): Former FBI director James Comey surrendered to authorities in Virginia on Wednesday after being charged with threatening President Donald Trump's life with a social media post.
Comey, an outspoken critic of the US president who faced other federal charges that were subsequently thrown out, posted a photo to Instagram in May 2025 of seashells arranged in the shape of the numbers 86 and 47 on a beach in North Carolina.
Trump said this gesture was a coded threat.
Comey faces one count of "willfully making a threat to take the life of and to inflict bodily harm upon the president of the United States" and another of making an interstate threat.
Each charge carries a maximum penalty of 10 years in prison.
The former FBI director appeared for a hearing in Alexandria, Virginia, where he did not enter a plea and was later allowed to leave.
Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, speaking to reporters, said he did not know when Comey's next court appearance would be.
Blanche defended the indictment when asked if anyone who posted the numbers "8647" would face charges.
"Every threats case is different," he said.
"Every time there is a threat against the president it doesn't necessarily lead to an indictment.
"It depends on the investigation. It depends on all kinds of factors," he said.
Comey apologised at the time for the Instagram post and said he "didn't realise some folks associate those numbers with violence."
"It never occurred to me but I oppose violence of any kind so I took the post down," he said.
In September, Comey was charged with making false statements to Congress and obstructing a congressional proceeding in what was widely seen as retribution by the Republican president against a political opponent.
A federal judge threw out that case on the grounds the prosecutor handpicked by Trump who brought the charges was unlawfully appointed.
Democrats are viewing this latest indictment in a similar light.
Senator Dick Durbin called the charges "baseless" and "petty retribution."
"This is another case of a weaponised Justice Department lashing out on behalf of a vengeful president," Durbin said in a statement.
Comey served as FBI director from 2013 to 2017.
During his tenure, he oversaw the investigation of Hillary Clinton's use of a private email server and the investigation of Russian interference in the 2016 election, among other notable cases.
Comey was fired by Trump in May 2017. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: London police probe 'terror' incident after two Jewish men stabbed
Article:
LONDON: Two Jewish men were wounded in stabbings in north London on Wednesday, investigated by police in the British capital as a "terrorist incident", after a series of antisemitic attacks in recent weeks.
King Charles III and Prime Minister Keir Starmer led the condemnation of the "appalling" late morning assaults in Golders Green, which is home to a large Jewish population.
Police said a 45-year-old man – a British national born in Somalia – was arrested on suspicion of attempted murder.
The two victims – aged 76 and 34 – were in a stable condition in hospital. The suspect, who also tried to stab police officers, was detained after initially being taken to hospital but discharged.
London's Metropolitan Police said he is believed to have been involved in another incident on Wednesday morning in southeast London in which one person suffered minor injuries.
In Golders Green, Met commissioner Mark Rowley, who faced shouts of "Shame on You" from a few hecklers, said the suspect had "a history of serious violence and mental health issues."
A little-known group believed to be linked to Iran, and which has claimed responsibility for the London arson attacks, said one of its "lone wolves" was behind the stabbings, the SITE Intelligence Group reported.
Harakat Ashab al-Yamin al-Islamiya (HAYI) – meaning The Islamic Movement of the People of the Right Hand – made the uncorroborated claim in a video posted online, according to SITE.
EMERGENCY MEETING
Local resident Moshie, 20, told AFP he had been "shocked" by the incident and said he felt like violence was "escalating."
"Whenever I look around I have one thing in mind. Does this person have a knife? That's our life now," he said.
Starmer convened an emergency meeting of senior ministers and officials, telling broadcasters later he would meet Thursday with "criminal justice agencies to make sure that we have effective and swift justice in these cases."
"The government is taking action in relation to security, cohesion, extremism," he added.
"But of course it's our responsibility to co-ordinate the immediate response here to this appalling attack, to ensure security is in place."
'HORRIFIC'
King Charles III, who is on a state visit to the United States, said he was "deeply concerned" in a statement issued by Buckingham Palace.
The stabbings follow a spate of arson attacks on synagogues and other Jewish sites in north London in recent weeks that have heightened security fears.
"After attacks on synagogues, Jewish institutions, community ambulances and now Jews targeted in Golders Green, the UK government can no longer claim this is under control," Israel's foreign ministry said on X.
The incident unfolded shortly after 11am (1000 GMT), when the Shomrim Jewish neighbourhood watch group first reported that a knife-wielding man was "attempting to stab Jewish members of the public."
Shomrim members helped detain the suspect before police arrived and subdued him using an electroshock weapon. The response of the Jewish volunteers earned widespread praise.
The victims were also treated at the scene by Hatzola, a Jewish volunteer ambulance service.
The stabbings follow a deadly assault on a synagogue in Manchester last October on the Jewish holy day of Yom Kippur that killed two people and seriously injured three.
'INTOLERABLE'
"Why do we have to live in fear? We're doing our best to live our lives as normal as possible, but every day is a struggle," Shomrim volunteer Steven Bak told AFP.
About 150 people protested late Wednesday near the site of the attack and were addressed by Richard Tice, the deputy leader of anti-immigrant party Reform UK.
Ephraim Mirvis, Britain's chief rabbi, told reporters the community was "strong" and "resilient" but "such attacks need to stop now."
"Zero tolerance – that's what our community wants," he said.
The first London attack in late March saw four Hatzola ambulances set ablaze.
Other incidents include an attack on a synagogue in Kenton, northwest London, and the premises of a Jewish charity. Another synagogue in Finchley, north London, was targeted last week.
Police have arrested 28 people in total over the various attacks – including Wednesday's – since the start of the US-Israeli war on Iran on February 28.
HAYI has claimed responsibility for many of them, and a spate of similar incidents across Europe.
Monitoring groups have reported a surge in antisemitic and Islamophobic incidents in Britain, particularly since the start of the war between Israel and Hamas in Gaza. -- AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Umno respects BN assemblymen's stance, backs Negri Sembilan unity govt
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Umno respects the decision of Negri Sembilan BN assemblymen who have voiced a loss of confidence in Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Aminuddin Harun.
However, the party maintains that it will continue to support the state's unity government to ensure stability and safeguard the welfare of the people.
Umno secretary-general Datuk Dr Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki said the party leadership had received a comprehensive briefing from Negri Sembilan Umno Liaison Committee chairman Datuk Seri Jalaluddin Alias regarding the matter.
The loss of confidence is reportedly linked to allegations that Aminuddin had disregarded the state's customary institutions and the Negri Sembilan State Constitution.
In light of this, Asyraf called for an immediate meeting between the Umno and PH political bureaus to resolve issues involving the core principles of the respective parties.
"This is in line with Clause 5(c) of the Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) on the formation of the unity government, which requires all disputed policy matters to be resolved within the Consultative Council," he told Harian Metro.
Asyraf said that the party remains steadfast in its adherence to Clause 3.1 of the Umno constitution, which pledges to defend the Malay Rulers as the pillar of national sovereignty.
"Umno views the latest developments involving the crisis surrounding the royal and customary institutions of Negri Sembilan seriously.
"It has sparked significant polemics and serious misunderstandings among component parties forming the unity government in Negri Sembilan," he added.
He said the stance was finalised following an Umno Political Bureau meeting held on Monday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Elderly couple killed after car crashes into drain
Article:
JEMPOL: An elderly couple died after their car veered into a drain along Jalan Kuala Pilah-Simpang Pertang here last night.
Jempol police chief Superintendent Norhisham Mustapar said the victims, a 75-year-old man and his 65-year-old wife, were travelling in a Proton Saga towards Kuala Pilah.
"The driver (the husband) is believed to have lost control of the vehicle before it veered into the opposite lane and crashed into a drain.
"Both victims sustained severe head injuries and died at the scene," he said in a statement.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Auditor resignations and opinion shopping
Article:
In Malaysia, every now and then, we come across instances when external auditors resign due to fee disagreements. Such resignations create what is called 'casual vacancies'. In such instances, the Board is empowered to appoint the auditors for the remainder of the term till the next annual general meeting (AGM). The risk is clear. An auditor appointed by shareholders at an AGM resigns (due to fee disagreements) only to be replaced by an auditor chosen by the Board. Needless to say, late-stage resignations are significant red flags for governance and internal control failures. It does not bolster corporate governance and transparency.
The Hong Kong Remedy
Hong Kong-listed companies must now obtain shareholder approval to change auditors.
In an updated guidance issued on Apr 1, it has been mandated that companies appoint or remove auditors only at general meetings. To prevent auditor dismissals due to disagreement over fees, the HK bourse requires companies to disclose specific audit fees or ranges to prevent fee disputes from being used as a pretext for dismissal.
The move effectively closes a loophole that allowed boards to pressure auditors into resigning without immediate shareholder oversight. Under the new rules, any action by a company that triggers an auditor's resignation will be treated as an active removal, necessitating a formal vote.
The tightening comes as the HK regulators crack down on opinion shopping, a practice where companies pressure auditors to quit near year-end deadlines to appoint a more compliant replacement via a casual vacancy.
Forced resignations
The forced resignation of external auditors followed by "auditor shopping" is one of the more troubling governance failures in modern corporations. It strikes at the heart of financial reporting integrity and undermines the very purpose of an independent audit. While boards of directors are entrusted with safeguarding shareholder interests and ensuring transparency, instances where they pressure auditors to resign—only to replace them with more compliant firms—raise serious concerns about ethics, accountability, and the reliability of financial statements.
At its core, the relationship between a company and its external auditor is meant to be independent and objective. Auditors are appointed to provide reasonable assurance that financial statements are free from material misstatement, whether due to fraud or error. Their role is not to please management or the board, but to challenge assumptions, test evidence, and, where necessary, issue qualified or adverse opinions. When a board forces an auditor to resign, it often signals a breakdown in this independence—frequently because the auditor is unwilling to endorse aggressive accounting treatments or overlook questionable practices.
Auditor Shopping
Auditor shopping typically follows such resignations. This refers to the practice of seeking out an audit firm that is more likely to agree with management's preferred accounting treatments or to adopt a less rigorous audit approach. While not illegal per se, it becomes highly problematic when it is driven by the desire to circumvent scrutiny. In such cases, the board is no longer acting as a steward of governance but as an enabler of opacity.
The motivations behind these actions can vary. In some cases, companies may be under financial pressure and seek to present a more favourable financial position to investors, lenders, or regulators. In others, there may be deeper issues such as fraud, mismanagement, or internal control weaknesses that the incumbent auditor has identified or is likely to uncover. Rather than addressing these issues, the board may choose the easier—but far riskier—path of replacing the auditor.
Eroding Investor Confidence
This behaviour carries significant consequences. First and foremost, it erodes investor confidence. Financial statements are a primary source of information for stakeholders, and their credibility depends heavily on the perceived independence of the auditor. When an auditor resigns under contentious circumstances, it raises red flags. Investors may question whether the financial statements can be trusted, leading to declines in share price and increased cost of capital.
Regulatory Scrutiny
Secondly, it exposes the company to regulatory scrutiny. Many jurisdictions require detailed disclosures when auditors resign, including the reasons for the resignation and any disagreements with management. Regulators and stock exchanges often review such cases closely, particularly if there are indications of governance failures. In severe instances, this can lead to investigations, fines, or even delisting. But companies can refrain from giving the actual reasons by merely citing 'disagreement over fees' as a convenient way to avoid accountability.
A Race to the Bottom
Thirdly, auditor shopping can create a race to the bottom among audit firms. If firms perceive that taking a stricter stance will result in losing clients, there may be a temptation—especially among smaller firms—to be more accommodating. This undermines the overall quality of audits and weakens the profession's credibility. It also places ethical firms at a competitive disadvantage, which is detrimental to the long-term health of the audit ecosystem.
Ultimately, forcing auditors to resign and engaging in auditor shopping reflects a failure of tone at the top. It suggests that the board prioritises short-term image management over long-term integrity. Such behaviour may offer temporary relief from scrutiny, but it often leads to greater problems down the line, including reputational damage and financial loss.
Strong corporate governance demands the opposite approach. Boards should view auditors as partners in accountability, not obstacles to be removed. When auditors raise concerns, these should be addressed constructively, not suppressed. Only by upholding the principles of independence, transparency, and ethical conduct can boards fulfil their fiduciary duties and maintain the trust of stakeholders.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UAE's Opec breakaway to reshape supply dynamics
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The global oil market is expected to become more fragmented and less coordinated following the United Arab Emirates' (UAE) decision to exit the Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (Opec), according to economists.
Crude oil prices spiked in an immediate, knee-jerk reaction after reports emerged that the UAE had exited both Opec and the wider Opec+ alliance.
IPPFA Sdn Bhd director of investment strategy and country economist Mohd Sedek Jantan said Opec has historically operated as a cartel that relies on coordinated production management to stabilise prices.
However, he noted that such stability can only be sustained when the collective benefits outweigh the incentives for individual members to break from agreed quotas.
"The UAE's move is economically rational, given its aggressive production expansion plans.
"It has invested heavily to raise output capacity from about 3.4 million barrels per day to nearly five million barrels per day, but Opec quota limits have restricted its ability to fully benefit from that capacity.
"This could increase price volatility in the short term while reshaping long-term supply dynamics," he told Business Times.
Mohd Sedek said oil prices may not fall immediately but volatility is likely to rise.
At around US$111 per barrel now, any additional UAE supply could place downward pressure on prices, although output increases are expected to be gradual.
He said the UAE's departure, as Opec's third-largest producer and one of its most flexible suppliers, would weaken the group's ability to manage supply and support prices.
As a result, price swings may become more frequent, driven by supply changes, market uncertainty and higher risk premiums.
Over the longer term, Mohd Sedek said the oil market could shift towards a looser structure without formal coordination, led by major producers such as Saudi Arabia, the US, Russia and the UAE.
"This may lead to higher overall production and structurally lower prices, benefiting importing countries but putting pressure on oil-exporting nations," he said.
He added that the UAE is likely to gradually lift production towards full capacity, strengthen direct trade ties with buyers in Asia and Europe, and use supply flexibility more strategically.
There is potential for the country to diversify oil trade settlement currencies, including greater use of the petroyuan, which could gradually affect the long-term dominance of the petrodollar.
For Malaysia, Mohd Sedek said the impact would be mixed, as lower and more volatile oil prices could reduce government income from petroleum-related dividends and taxes, thereby narrowing fiscal space.
However, he said cheaper energy could support manufacturing margins, boost consumer spending, reduce subsidy pressures and improve Malaysia's appeal to energy-intensive industries.
He added that Petronas may also accelerate efforts to improve efficiency, expand liquefied natural gas (LNG) operations and advance cleaner energy transition strategies.
Universiti Teknologi Mara Business Management Faculty senior lecturer Dr Mohamad Idham Md Razak said the UAE's exit from Opec and Opec+ could introduce greater uncertainty into global oil price stability, primarily because it weakens collective discipline over production quotas.
"Opec's strength has historically relied on coordinated supply management. Any fragmentation reduces its ability to smooth price volatility during shocks.
"If the UAE opts to increase production independently, it may contribute to short-term price fluctuations, either through downward pressure from higher supply or upward volatility if coordination breaks down further," he noted.
According to him, the development may signal a gradual weakening of Opec's influence, although not an immediate collapse.
He said the real implication lies in the precedent it sets. If other members follow suit, the cartel's ability to act as a price stabiliser would diminish.
"In such a scenario, non-Opec producers such as the US shale industry and large consuming nations could gain from more flexible supply conditions.
"Conversely, countries heavily reliant on coordinated pricing to sustain revenues may face greater income uncertainty, especially during periods of oversupply or demand shocks," he said.
For Malaysia, Mohamad Idham said increased global oil price volatility would have mixed but manageable implications.
He said on one hand, higher prices could support government revenue from petroleum-related sources.
On the other hand, they would place greater strain on fuel subsidies, potentially widening the fiscal deficit if prices remain elevated.
"From an inflation perspective, volatility rather than just high prices is the bigger concern, as it complicates planning for businesses and may lead to inconsistent cost pressures filtering into transport and food prices.
"Overall, Malaysia would need to rely more on flexible fiscal management and targeted subsidies to balance the trade-off between revenue gains and cost containment," he noted.
The UAE has announced its exit from Opec and its allies (Opec+) effective May 1, citing national interests and long-term energy strategy after nearly 60 years of membership.
The move is seen as a significant blow to the oil-producing group amid an ongoing energy shock linked to the conflict involving Iran.
Several members have exited Opec in recent years including Indonesia (2016), Qatar (2019), Ecuador (2020) and Angola (2023).
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Quiet Tanah Merah resort unmasked as love scam call centre in police raid
Article:
TANAH MERAH: Residents near a resort raided for a love scam syndicate expressed shock that the site served as a front for criminal activity.
Locals said  the resort had been operating for  years and had never drawn suspicion.
Food stall worker Aina Ghazali said she was stunned when she learnt about the raid.
"I pass by the area almost every day and never imagined these things were happening there. It looked normal from the outside.
"I only found out after watching the news on television," said the 24-year-old.
A trader, who wants be known only as Yacob, also said  the resort did not attract much attention.
"It didn't look busy. There were not many customers, so we did not suspect anything unusual.
"We can't blame the resort management as they might not have been aware of the syndicate's activities until it was uncovered by  police," said the 52-year-old.
A security guard, who declined to be named, said the situation was not entirely surprising.
"The resort has not been doing well for some time. Business seemed slow, so maybe the operator rented it out," he said.
Police raided the resort on April 22 and arrested 58 people, all but two of whom were foreigners.
The group, which targeted Singaporean men, had taken up several rooms and a hall.
Police  seized 46 desktop computers, five laptops, 118 mobile phones and several  routers used to ensure stable and high-speed Internet connection.
Kelantan police chief Datuk Mohd Yusoff Mamat said  investigations found that all 58 were lured by promises of lucrative income.
He said the love scam syndicate, believed to have been operating for about a month since late March, used women posing as Indonesians to  manipulate victims before persuading them to transfer money.
"Their modus operandi was to initiate contact through messages, build relationships via WhatsApp without video calls, and eventually deceive the victims into sending them money," he said.
Police said the operation was run daily from noon to midnight, and managed by three supervisors and supported by dozens of operators.
Investigations revealed that 33 foreign nationals had valid travel documents.
Yusoff said the 53 men and five women  were remanded to assist in investigations.
A check at the resort  found that it remained open but was operating with only a small number of customers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Departing US still owes money, says WHO chief
Article:
GENEVA: The United States has still not paid off its membership fee arrears at the World Health Organisation, the WHO chief said Wednesday, with Washington's intention to leave conditional on paying up.
The UN health agency's director-general Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus said he hoped the United States would come up with the money they owe – a self-imposed condition for quitting the organisation.
US President Donald Trump, on his first day back in office in January 2025, handed the WHO his country's one-year withdrawal notice.
"In terms of the arrears from the US, the US withdrawal is conditioned with two things," Tedros told a press conference with the UN correspondents' association ACANU.
"One is notification one year in advance, which is actually met. And the second is paying the arrears, so we hope they will do that but we haven't received anything yet."
The United States was the biggest contributor to the WHO budget.
Tedros said there were "no signals" indicating that Washington would come up with the cash.
But he added: "To be honest, it's not about the money.
"The issue is health security needs universality and the US, by withdrawing, makes itself unsafe and makes the rest of the world unsafe. So it's lose-lose.
"So our focus is not on the money. The focus is on helping the US to understand and reconsider.
"Where there is a vacuum, the virus wins. It's as simple as that.
"It's global cooperation and solidarity which is the best response."
Though the US flag no longer flies outside the WHO headquarters in Geneva, the WHO's decision-making body – the annual assembly of member states – will decide upon the US withdrawal when it meets from May 18-23.
The WHO constitution does not include a withdrawal clause.
But the United States reserved the right to withdraw when it joined the WHO in 1948 – on condition of giving one year's notice and meeting its financial obligations in full for that fiscal year.
The notice period has now expired but Washington has still not paid its 2024 or 2025 dues, owing around $260 million.
The United States was traditionally the biggest donor to the WHO.
In January, as the notice expired, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio and US Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. attacked the WHO, claiming it had "tarnished and trashed everything that America has done for it", with "the insults to America" continuing to the end.
"The reverse is true," the WHO responded.
Despite the parting shot from Kennedy, the US health secretary still speaks with Tedros on a regular basis.
"We keep in touch every now and then," the WHO chief told reporters. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sabah leads nation in maritime arrests, inspections and search operations: MMEA
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Sabah has become the country's maritime enforcement hotspot, recording  the highest number of inspections, searches and arrests by the Malaysian Maritime Enforcement Agency (MMEA) over the past two years.
MMEA director-general Datuk Mohd Rosli Abdullah said the trend reflected the  persistent threats Sabah faced  from foreign vessel intrusion, smuggling and immigration offences.
"MMEA conducted 386,205 inspections on ships and boats nationwide using visual checks, voice communication and radar surveillance in 2024 and 2025," he said.
"Of the total, 191,707 inspections were carried out in 2024, and 194,498  in 2025."
He said Sabah  accounted for 60,209 inspections in 2024, the highest in the country, followed by Johor (18,755) and aerial operations (18,683).
"In the same year,  51,924 search operations were conducted, with Sabah again topping the list with 28,629 cases.
"In terms of arrests, Sabah also led with 400 cases, followed by Kedah and Perlis (209 cases) and Perak (191 cases).
"Cases under the Fisheries Act 1985 dominated  with 685 cases, followed by   the Immigration Act 1959/1963 (281 cases) and  Customs Act 1967 (61 cases)."
Rosli said enforcement momentum continued into 2025, with 52,810 search operations conducted at sea.
He said 1,373 arrests  were recorded, with offences under the Fisheries Act 1985 remaining the highest at 547 cases.
"This was followed by cases under the Merchant Shipping Ordinance 1952 (311 cases) and the Immigration Act 1959/1963 (223 cases).
"The (Sabah Ports and Harbours Enactment) recorded 129 cases, followed by the Control of Supplies Act 1961 (69 cases) and the Customs Act 1967 (45 cases).
"Cases under the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952 accounted for 18 cases, while other offences involving various laws totalled 31."
Rosli said the arrests involved a wide range of offences, including encroachment by foreign fishing vessels, smuggling of controlled and contraband goods, Immigration violations and breaches of fisheries laws.
"Overall, (MMEA) recorded 1,124 arrests in part of its 2025 operations, while 1,116 cases have been investigated across maritime states, with the majority involving offences under the Fisheries Act, followed by the Merchant Shipping Ordinance and the Immigration Act.
"These statistics reflect our continued commitment to strengthening maritime law enforcement and ensuring the safety and sovereignty of the nation's waters," he said.
[[nid:1429331]]
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Report, don't speculate: Sabah Labour Department warns against unverified child labour claims
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: A restaurant operator is hiring undocumented migrants, claiming high turnover among local workers.
The 48-year-old, who wished to remain anonymous, said his family business struggled to retain local staff.
"They quit very quickly, sometimes within months. In the end, we have no choice but to hire undocumented workers. They are more willing to work and complain less."
He said the restaurant operated from 6.30am to 3pm daily, with salaries based on the government's minimum wage guidelines.
The Sabah Labour Department said it was working to safeguard the welfare of the state's workforce, including non-resident employees.
In 2024, Sabah recorded 796,900 non-residents employed by 37,646 registered employers. These workers are protected  by laws including the Anti-Trafficking in Persons and Anti-Smuggling of Migrants Act 2007.
Most non-resident workers are Indonesians (90.2 per cent), followed by Filipinos (7.9 per cent), Nepalese (0.1 per cent) and Cambodians (0.1 per cent).
The agriculture sector remains one of Sabah's largest employers, with 591,800 workers, of whom 58 per cent (344,700) are non-citizens, mainly in oil palm plantations.
Sabah Labour Department director Wan Zulkifli Wan Setapa urged the public to report child labour practices.
"We are the enforcement body authorised to investigate such cases," he said.
"Making general claims without action will not solve the issue and may lead to misunderstanding or exploitation of the matter," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: A classroom under a tree: Sabah's stateless children learning without schools or documents
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The morning quiet along a city street is broken by children singing in Mandarin — but they are not Chinese, nor do they speak the language at home.
Under a large tree, undocumented children learn to speak and write and do arithmetic in informal classes run by volunteers on alternate days.
They attend such "classes" diligently as their mothers sit nearby.
One volunteer, who wishes to be known only as "Lucy", said teaching even basic English, Bahasa Melayu and Chinese was challenging because the children lacked a conducive environment to practise.
"They are quite bright and can pick up Chinese words from songs and conversations," said the 69-year-old, who occasionally brings toys and treats.
"I don't have high academic expectations, but I hope what they learn will help them in the future."
She added that the children were the third generation in the state without legal documents.
"It is a vicious cycle. Without documents, they have little hope of improving their lives," she said. "Men may find work in construction or manual labour, but what about the women? That is why I teach them English, Chinese and Bahasa Melayu."
Lucy said the public could also help by educating these stateless children informally.
She said she was inspired by a representative from the United Nations Children's Fund who once used natural materials such as rocks and branches as teaching aids.
"It means anyone can teach children on the streets without needing extra resources," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Undocumented children in Sabah trapped by stigma and denied basic rights: NGO
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: Social stigma against undocumented children is hampering efforts to help them, says an activist.
Cameliah Thomas, community engagement lead for Advocates for Non-discrimination and Access to Knowledge (Anak), said the public often sees these children as troublemakers or potential criminals, making them reluctant to offer support.
"Sadly, society often sees them as beggars or a nuisance, instead of children who need protection and education," she told the New Straits Times.
[[nid:1429366]]
"These children did not choose this life. But the system pushes them into the shadows because no documents mean no access to formal schooling."
Anak, a Sabah-based grassroots non-profit organisation, provides paralegal aid on civil registration, advocates for stateless and immigration issues, researches marginalised communities, runs health and human rights campaigns, and distributes food and health supplies to underserved groups.
Cameliah said most street children they work with come from stateless communities, particularly the Bajau Laut along Sabah's coasts and islands.
The lack of identification documents excludes them from education, healthcare and social protection.
"In such conditions, they are forced onto the streets, begging or selling small items, and many risk exploitation.
"That is why we run awareness programmes to remind communities that these children are not criminals, but victims of circumstance."
Anak is urging the government to treat stateless and street children as a pressing human rights issue.
Its demands include ensuring access to education regardless of citizenship, recognising alternative education centres, and providing solutions for identity documentation.
"We want the government to embody the principle of 'no child left behind'," she said. "No child should be trapped in poverty because of a citizenship quandary not of their doing."
The state government set up a temporary protection centre in the state capital in 2023 to provide  basic education, among others.
In May last year, Kota Kinabalu City Hall signed a memorandum of understanding with the United Nations Children's Fund to enhance child safety and access to quality education.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Jack', still only 12, toils to keep family afloat
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: A young boy emerged quietly from beneath a table when a market vendor called out for help carrying groceries.
Though only 12, Jack (not his real name) — small for his age and wearing a loose shirt — has worked in the central market here long enough to understand its daily rhythm.
He kept his gaze on the concrete floor as he followed the stranger through the morning bustle. His fee depends on a customer's generosity, though it rarely falls below RM2 per job, regardless of the load.
"On average, I earn around RM30 a day, working from 3am to 5pm," he said.
His father also takes on odd jobs in the city.
Jack lives on Gaya Island, a 10-minute boat ride from the mainland. The return trip on a small passenger boat costs RM6, a necessary daily expense.
He eats twice most days: once at the market and again at home.
From his daily earnings, RM5 goes towards a smartphone purchased on instalment that he shares with his four siblings.
Jack is the third child in the family. His eldest brother, 15, helps with odd jobs closer to home while the youngest is 2 years old.
Jack is one of about 20 children aged between 8 and 12 working a similar job at the market.
"Thus, I don't feel alone," he said.
The work is backbreaking and fraught with hidden dangers.
"We have to be careful of certain adults who will try to extort money from us."
Unlike others in more touristy areas, Jack and his friends do not beg.
"We carry things. We work."
There was a time when the authorities brought Jack to a community centre, where he learnt about responsibility and earning an honest living.
"I know the authorities won't be too happy if they caught us sniffing glue, fighting in the streets or causing trouble," he said.
Asked about health, Jack said he missed the Covid-19 vaccination programme because he was working.
"I'm not sure if I've received any other vaccinations," he added. "When I'm sick, I just rest at home."
He has no official documents to stay in Malaysia, though he speaks fluent Bahasa Melayu and can read and do basic maths.
Asked about school, he said: "Maybe one day, I'll have the chance to study like other children."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: From sea gypsies to street kids: Sabah's stateless children fighting to survive
Article:
KOTA KINABALU:  Last year, a video of migrant children grabbing leftovers from a table at a restaurant went viral, shocking netizens.
But for many in Sabah, these "street kids" are a common sight.
These undocumented children roam the streets here, some begging or waiting outside banks in the hope of handouts, while others earn meagre incomes collecting cans and bottles for recycling.
Some sniff glue to stave off hunger.
These street kids, who speak a dialect similar to Bajau Ubian, have been around for decades, even if social media makes their presence appear new.
Sabah Welfare Department director Jais Asri said these children, most of whom are of Bajau Laut descent, will continue to roam Sabah's streets unless practical, long-term measures are put in place.
"These people can be been found in waters off Sabah, Kudat, Kunak, Lahad Datu and Semporna.
"They have long relied on the sea for their livelihood. But with their space shrinking due to curfews in the Eastern Sabah Security Zone (ESSZone) and the expansion of marine parks, where else can they go?" he told the New Straits Times.
ESSZone, which covers 10 coastal districts, was established in March 2013 following the Lahad Datu intrusion by armed militants from the southern Philippines.
It was established to prevent future threats, such as cross-border incursions, piracy and kidnappings.
While ESSZone has exacerbated the issue, the street kids are symptomatic of Sabah's wider undocumented migrant problem.
The decades-old issue stems from the displacement of many Bajau Laut, or sea gypsies, due to post-colonial borders, conflict and dwindling resources and opportunities.
They are a maritime ethnic group  known for their traditional, nomadic, boat-dwelling lifestyle. These expert free-divers and fishermen have lived on the waters off Malaysia, Indonesia and the Philippines for centuries but are largely stateless.
Members of this community in Sabah mainly originate from waters off Semporna, with some holding identification documents marked "Palauh", issued by the Semporna district chief in the 1990s.
Having been displaced onto land, many turn to illegal work to survive. For the children, this often means begging.
"Alternatively, some men work on large fishing vessels, which means being away from their families for months. In their absence, the children are left to help support their mothers and siblings," he said.
[[nid:1429351]]
SEARCH FOR SOLUTION
The state government has set up temporary protection centres for non-citizen beggars in Kota Kinabalu, Tawau, Sandakan and Lahad Datu.
For example, the centre here took in 298 children from 2023 to June last year, with periods of one week to three months each.
"There are structured programmes in place, including self-care, community living and informal spiritual education. After this period, they are returned to their parents or guardians under specific conditions," said Jais.
However, the department faces constraints in facilities, trained personnel and expertise in providing social and psychological support.
As a result, a six-agency task force was formed, involving the welfare department, the Sabah Affairs and Research Office, local governments, police, Immigration, and the Security and Emergency Assistance Agency.
Jais said the department was also running the "Bijak Membantu, Elak Tertipu" (Help Wisely, Don't Be Deceived) awareness campaign to discourage donating to child beggars.
"Giving money to these children only perpetuates the begging culture and increases the risk of exploitation. Instead, the public should channel donations to legitimate protection centres or non-governmental organisations.
"They should also report cases of child exploitation or begging syndicates to the police, our department or local authorities."
However, Jais acknowledged these were only temporary measures.
"Without proper documentation, sea gypsies and their descendants risk remaining trapped in a cycle of deprivation on land."
He proposed a more sustainable solution: allocating marine zones where these communities can return to their traditional way of life.
"There should be no prejudice in dealing with sea gypsies. They deserve to be treated as human beings, just like everyone else.
"Their nomadic existence in Sabah's waters is part of our cultural richness and should be acknowledged, not erased."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran war has cost US military US$25bil so far - Pentagon
Article:
WASHINGTON: The war against Iran has cost the US military US$25 billion since it was launched in late February, a senior Pentagon official said Wednesday.
"We're spending about $25 billion on Operation Epic Fury. Most of that is in munitions," acting Pentagon comptroller Jules Hurst told lawmakers, using the official name for US operation.
US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth later told the same congressional hearing that the estimated figure was less than US$25 billion at this point.
The Pentagon chief pushed back against questions about the war's cost, saying: "The question I would ask this committee is, what is it worth to ensure that Iran never gets a nuclear weapon?"
The United States and Israel launched a massive air campaign against Iran's military and leadership on Feb 28.
US President Donald Trump has indefinitely extended what was an initial two-week ceasefire that brought a halt to the fighting, but the conflict – and its widespread economic fallout – remains unresolved. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MetMalaysia issues Level 1 heat alert for 17 areas
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
THE Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) has placed 17 areas nationwide under Level 1 hot weather alert.
They include the whole of Perlis and the Kubang Pasu, Pendang, Kota Setar and Padang Terap districts in Kedah.
In Perak, the areas placed on alert are Kuala Kangsar and Hulu Perak, while in Kelantan, they are Jeli, Pasir Mas, Tanah Merah and Machang.
The alert also covers Kuala Lumpur, as well as the Gombak and Sepang districts in Selangor.
In Sabah, Beaufort and Telupid are on the list, while in Sarawak, Limbang is affected.
MetMalaysia said the Level 1 alert is issued when the daily maximum temperature reaches between 35°C and 37°C for at least three consecutive days.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: GOF seizes RM1m in untaxed timber, equipment
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
THE General Operations Force (GOF) raided a timber factory in Chuchoh Puteri near here and seized more than RM1 million in assets, including untaxed timber.
Tuesday's raid under Op Khazanah Bersepadu was conducted in collaboration with the Forestry Department.
GOF Southeast Brigade commander Senior Assistant Commissioner Ahmad Radzi Hussain said seven men aged 22 to 51 were also detained.
"We found several people operating heavy machinery and cutting timber during the raid.
"The factory clerk produced a forest timber transfer permit that appeared suspicious. We believe that the timber at the premises is also untaxed.
"We seized heavy machinery, 141 logs, 3,035 processed timber pieces, and other equipment worth more than RM1 million," he said in a statement yesterday.
Radzi said the clerk, who is the younger brother of the factory owner, told authorities that the factory bought untaxed forest timber from local residents at RM12 per foot.
"The timber is processed and then sold to agents for the Kuala Lumpur market at RM700 per tonne.
"The case is being investigated under Section 84 of the National Forestry Act 1984," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: SkyeChip to raise RM352m via IPO
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: SkyeChip Bhd is expected to raise up to RM352 million from its listing on the Main Market of Bursa Malaysia on May 20.
According to its prospectus released yesterday, RM211.5 million, or 60.1 per cent of the proceeds, will be used for research and development of integrated circuit (IC) products and silicon intellectual property (IP).
A further RM57.1 million (16.2 per cent of the proceeds) will be used to expand the company's operational facilities and computing infrastructure. RM36.7 million will be allocated for subscription and licensing, while RM32.4 million will go towards working capital.
The remaining RM14.3 million is earmarked for listing expenses.
SkyeChip, a semiconductor design company, has fixed its initial public offering (IPO) price at 88 sen per share and will issue 400 million new shares.
This comprises an institutional offering of 264.7 million shares and a retail offering of 135.3 million shares.
Of the retail portion, 35.9 million shares will be offered to the Malaysian public, with 50 per cent reserved for Bumiputera investors, while the remainder will be allocated to eligible persons.
The listing is expected to be the largest front-end semiconductor IPO in Asean.
Deputy Investment, Trade and Industry Minister Sim Tze Tzin, who attended the event, said SkyeChip's market debut reflects Malaysia's push to move up the semiconductor value chain, strengthening its position in advanced manufacturing and high-value chip development.
The prospectus launch marks a major milestone.
It formalises SkyeChip's transition from a privately held semiconductor design firm into a public market contender and signals its next phase of growth as an emerging Malaysian deep-tech chip company.
SkyeChip chief executive officer Datuk Fong Swee Kiang said: "The listing represents a significant addition to Malaysia's public markets and is fully aligned with the nation's ambition to elevate the semiconductor sector into a global powerhouse."
Applications for the retail tranche will close on May 6.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NZ officials reject statue remembering Japan's sex slaves
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
NEW ZEALAND officials yesterday rejected an application to install a statue commemorating so-called "comfort women" enslaved by Japan before and during World War 2 after Tokyo suggested it could harm diplomatic relations.
Japan forced up to 200,000 women from Korea, China and Southeast Asia into sexual slavery from 1932 until 1945 and the issue remains a sore point in Tokyo's relations with its neighbours.
The Korean Garden Trust had sought to install a statue honouring the survivors at Barry's Point Reserve in the Auckland suburb of Takapuna.
But after public consultation, the Devonport-Takapuna Local Board declined an application to install the statue.
"This was a difficult decision, and one we did not make lightly," said board chair Trish Deans. "We carefully considered staff advice and the feedback received from the community through a formal consultation process."
Among the submissions was a letter from Japanese Ambassador Makoto Osawa, which claimed it could "cause division and conflict within New Zealand's wonderful multi-ethnic and multicultural society and between Japanese and Korean communities".
Wellington's Foreign Affairs and Trade Ministry also said the Japanese government had made "formal representations" about the proposed statue.
Deans said many submitters had supported the statue as an opportunity to learn and reflect on what happened during the war.
"We recognise the significance of the history the statue represents, and we acknowledge the survivors whose stories it seeks to honour," she added. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Motorcyclist to be charged today over viral expressway road rage video
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  A man caught on a viral video allegedly
kicking a car
at the Kajang-Lekas Highway toll plaza on April 26 will be charged in court today, following the completion of police investigations.
Kajang police confirmed that investigation papers on the case have been completed and prosecution has been instructed to proceed.
"The suspect will be charged at the Bandar Baru Bangi Magistrate's Court on April 30, 2026, at 9am under Section 427 of the Penal Code for mischief," District police chief Assistant Commissioner Naazron Abdul Yusof said in a statement.
If convicted, the offence carries a jail term of not less than one year and up to five years, or a fine, or both.
"In addition to the criminal charge, the suspect has also been issued a compound under Rule 8(1) of the Road Traffic Rules 1959 for failing to keep to the proper lane," he added.
Meanwhile, the driver of the motorcar involved in the incident, who was also the complainant, has not been spared action. He has been issued a summons under Rule 6(1) of the same regulations for overtaking and obstructing another vehicle from overtaking, believed to have contributed to the incident.
Police reminded road users to strictly adhere to traffic laws and regulations, stressing that responsible driving behaviour is essential to prevent accidents and maintain public order.
"The Kajang police will continue to take firm action against any individual who violates the law and endangers themselves or other road users," the statement said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Only Elon Musk can fire Elon Musk from SpaceX, filing shows
Article:
NEW YORK: SpaceX is telling investors that no one can fire Elon Musk from his role as chief executive and chairman of the board without the billionaire founder's consent, according to an excerpt of its IPO filing reviewed by Reuters.
The filing states that Musk "can only be removed from our board or these positions by the vote of Class B holders" - super-voting shares with ten votes apiece that he will control after the IPO, making his removal effectively a self-vote.
If he "retains a significant portion of his holdings of Class B common stock for an extended period of time, he could continue to control the election and removal of a majority of our board."
The provision sits on top of a dual-class framework SpaceX plans to adopt at its IPO, a common setup among founder-led tech companies going public that gives founders and early investors greater control relative to public shareholders.
But even in those structures, boards typically retain formal authority to remove a CEO, even if founders can steer outcomes through voting power.
The full impact of the provision would depend on details in SpaceX's founding legal documents, corporate governance experts said.
Taken together, the provisions would give Musk an effective veto over any attempt to remove him, a level of control experts say goes beyond the norm by tying removal directly to his own voting power.
SpaceX warned prospective investors that the structure "will limit or preclude your ability to influence corporate matters and the election of our directors."
"This provision is not common. Usually removal of the CEO is a decision left to the board, and controllers rely on their power to replace the board," said Lucian Bebchuk, a Harvard Law School professor whose research focuses on corporate governance, law and finance.
SpaceX and Musk didn't respond to requests for comment.
Dual-class share structures have become a standard feature of founder-led technology companies going public in recent years.
Facebook, which listed in 2012, gave super-voting shares to pre-IPO holders including Mark Zuckerberg, though voting power later concentrated as early investors sold down their stakes.
More recent listings, including Figma, have concentrated super-voting shares more directly in founders after an IPO.
SpaceX will be split into Class A common stock for public investors and Class B super-voting shares for insiders.
Musk will hold a majority of the voting power, tying board control and executive authority directly to shares he controls, Reuters previously reported.
The arrangement represents a departure from Tesla, which has a single share class.
SpaceX is incorporated in Texas, following Tesla, which Musk shifted there after a Delaware court voided his US$56 billion pay package for running the automaker.
The compensation package was reinstated by the Delaware Supreme Court late last year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PNB tipped for leadership change, Abdul Rahman 'set' to leave
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Permodalan Nasional Bhd (PNB) may soon begin the search for a new president and group chief executive.
Sources said incumbent Datuk Abdul Rahman Ahmad has decided to leave the country's largest fund manager after less than two years in the role.
Abdul Rahman is believed to be stepping away for personal reasons.
While his contract is due to expire in June, sources said he is not keen in renewing and that it is still unclear whether the government is willing to let him go.
The potential exit and eventual appointment of a successor will be under the microscope given PNB's stature as one of the nation's largest government-linked funds, with assets under management exceeding RM300 billion.
Abdul Rahman's potential exit comes at a time when PNB is focused on expanding its global footprint and modernising its digital platforms to reach younger investors.
PNB has sizeable stakes in many of Malaysia's blue-chip corporates such as Malayan Banking Bhd and stewardship of flagship savings products such as Amanah Saham Bumiputera.
The fund recently reported a cumulative distribution since inception of RM279 billion to its unitholders as at Dec 31 2025, underscoring its role as a bedrock for Malaysian retail savings.
In 2025, its unit Amanah Saham Nasional Bhd distributed a total of RM15.3 billion across all 18 unit trust funds, the highest annual total ever recorded.
Abdul Rahman only returned to PNB officially on July 1 2024, having previously led the institution from 2016 to 2019 before moving to CIMB Group.
His departure, if confirmed, will trigger a high-stakes search for a successor capable of navigating PNB's global multi-asset strategy amid a challenging investment landscape.
Business Times has reached out to him for comment.
Policymakers are expected to prioritise a candidate who combines institutional credibility, capital markets experience and the ability to steer PNB through a more challenging global investment climate.
The Finance Ministry and Yayasan Pelaburan Bumiputera (YPB), which directly oversees PNB, are expected to finalise a shortlist in the coming weeks.
A swift transition will be critical to maintain market confidence and ensure the execution of PNB's long-term sustainability goals, sources said.
Among names floating in financial circles is Bank Simpanan Nasional chief executive officer Datuk Jay Khairil Jeremy Abdullah, who has built a reputation as a steady operator within the government-linked financial ecosystem.
His experience in retail banking, financial inclusion and public-sector mandates is seen as relevant to PNB's dual commercial and nation-building objectives.
But if the Finance Ministry and YPB are to look internally, Datuk Rick Ramli would be the most immediate candidate.
The current deputy president and group chief executive plays a central role in PNB's strategic direction, focusing on pillars like expanding global investments and cultural transformation.
For now, attention will be on whether Abdul Rahman's exit is confirmed and who emerges as the frontrunner to take one of corporate Malaysia's most influential jobs.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police track individuals over late-night fireworks in Setia Alam
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Police are tracking down individuals believed to be involved in a case of disturbing the peace after fireworks were set off in Setia Alam past midnight, an incident that recently went viral on social media.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner Ramsay Embol said the fireworks were believed to have produced between 50 and 100 loud explosions in succession during a wedding photography session at the public parking area of the Setia Alam Community Trail.
He said the incident was recorded on April 26 at 1.05am, with fireworks set off in three consecutive bursts. A similar incident was also reported on April 27 at 2.11am.
"A police report was lodged on April 29 at 8.26am after the video went viral and attracted public attention," he said in a statement.
Ramsay said anyone with information on the case can contact the investigating officer, Inspector Siti Mahanira Mahat, at 019-252 2905 to assist investigations.
The case is being investigated under Section 13 of the Minor Offences Act 1955 and Section 7 of the Explosives Act 1957.
Earlier, a video circulating on social media showed a group of individuals in a parking area, believed to be for a wedding shoot, setting off fireworks near an apartment area in Setia Alam.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hajiji: No Sabah boundary change without assembly consent
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: No changes to Sabah's boundaries can be made without the consent of the State Legislative Assembly, says Chief Minister Datuk Seri Hajiji Noor, amid claims involving land on Sebatik Island.
He said the reported transfer of 100ha on the island to Indonesia did not constitute a surrender of sovereignty, but reflected compliance with the 1891 Boundary Convention.
"The land boundary on Sebatik Island is based on the 1891 Boundary Convention signed between the British and Dutch governments on June 20, 1891.
"Under Article 4, the boundary is set along the latitude of 4°10' North. This area was previously classified as an Outstanding Boundary Problem as it had not been finalised," he said in his winding-up speech at the state legislative assembly, which was read by his assistant minister Datuk Jonsiton Bangkuai.
Hajiji said a joint Malaysia–Indonesia technical review in 1983 found discrepancies between the boundary on the ground and that stipulated in the convention.
He said further investigation, demarcation and re-survey works were carried out between February and November 2019 using modern technology to ensure accurate alignment.
"The realignment was undertaken together with boundary adjustments at Sungai Sinapad and Sungai Sesai.
"As a result, Malaysia gained an additional 780ha of territory previously within Indonesian borders," he said.
He added that the boundaries at Sebatik Island, Sungai Sinapad and Sungai Sesai were finalised following the signing of a memorandum of understanding on Feb 18, 2025.
However, Hajiji said that the Malaysia–Indonesia land boundary had yet to be fully resolved, particularly in the intertidal zone on the eastern side of Sebatik Island, extending about 870m out to sea, as well as in four Outstanding Boundary Problem areas in the Sarawak–West Kalimantan sector.
He said the Sabah government had been actively involved at every stage of negotiations to safeguard the state's interests, with representation from the Chief Minister's Department, State Attorney-General's Chambers and the Lands and Surveys Department.
He added that the entire process was conducted based on mutual agreement between both countries and in accordance with international law, without involving compensation or reciprocal arrangements, while ensuring Sabah's rights and border security are upheld.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd
